answers:
**1. Root cause and fix location.**
The root cause is a field-name mismatch introduced in `config/scoring_config.yaml` vs. `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`. `pipeline/score_batch.py` reads `gold_field = config["expected_label_field"]` and then does `gold = record.get(gold_field)` for each eval record, comparing it to the model's prediction. `config/scoring_config.yaml` currently sets `expected_label_field: label`, but every record in `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` uses the key `gold_label`, not `label` (verified: `head -3 fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` shows `{"text": ..., "gold_label": "billing"}`). So `record.get("label")` returns `None` for every record, `prediction == None` is always `False`, and `statistics.mean(hits)` is always `0.0` — regardless of how good the actual predictions are.

The fix: change `config/scoring_config.yaml`, updating `expected_label_field: label` to `expected_label_field: gold_label` (a one-line config fix; no pipeline code needs to change).

**2. When it began and what introduced it.**
It began with commit `53551e8` ("chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)"), dated 2026-06-17 13:00:00 +0000 — matching `nightly_scores.csv`, where accuracy is 0.91 through 2026-06-16 and drops to 0.00 starting exactly on 2026-06-17 and every day since. That commit renamed the label field in both `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` (from `label` to `gold_label` on every record) and updated `REQUIRED_KEYS` in `score_batch.py` from `{"text", "label"}` to `{"text", "gold_label"}` — but it did **not** update `config/scoring_config.yaml`'s `expected_label_field`, which was left as `label`. The three-way contract between the fixture schema, the required-keys check, and the config value fell out of sync in this one commit.

**3. Is the "not reproducible / flaky" conclusion correct?**
No, and it's not just wrong, it was tested via a path that structurally cannot reproduce the bug. `pipeline/score_one.py` (the script used in the prior investigation) calls `predict_category(normalize_simple(text))` directly and prints the prediction — it never loads `config/scoring_config.yaml`, never reads `expected_label_field`, and never compares against any gold label from `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`. It only exercises the classifier, which is also confirmed deterministic and keyword-rule-based (`pipeline/classifier.py`, no model call, no randomness) — so there is no "model flakiness" mechanism available in this pipeline at all. The bug lives entirely in the batch scoring/comparison logic (`score_batch.py` + `scoring_config.yaml`), a code path `score_one.py` never touches. Re-running individual tickets by hand was guaranteed to "look correct" regardless of whether the bug was present, because it never checks accuracy against gold labels.

Correct reproduction: run the actual nightly path, `python -m pipeline.score_batch` (or call `score_batch.score()`), which loads the config, loads `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`, and computes accuracy the same way the nightly job does. Doing so reproduces 0.0 deterministically and immediately shows `gold = record.get(gold_field)` resolving to `None` — no flakiness, no timing dependence, 100% reproducible every run.

**4. Assessment of the proposed next step (roll back the model).**
Do not do this. The classifier isn't a versioned ML model that can regress in this repo — `pipeline/classifier.py` is a static, deterministic keyword-matching function, and it was untouched by the commit that broke scoring (`git log` shows no changes to `classifier.py` or `preprocess.py` around 2026-06-17). Rolling back "the model" would touch a component that isn't broken, would not fix the config/fixture mismatch, and would very likely leave the accuracy at 0.0 after the rollback too — at which point the team would probably (incorrectly) conclude the rollback also failed, wasting further investigation time. It also risks introducing an unrelated regression in actual classification quality for no benefit. The correct action is the one-line config fix in `config/scoring_config.yaml` described in Answer 1.
report:
Good, deterministic classifier confirms individual predictions are unaffected by this bug (it's purely a scoring-side comparison bug, not a prediction bug). Investigation complete.

## Plan
Determine why nightly batch accuracy fell from ~0.91 to 0.0 around three weeks ago in the ticket-classification pipeline, verify or refute the prior "not reproducible / flaky" conclusion, and evaluate the proposed remediation (rolling back the model). Followed the systematic-debugging-playbook: checked prior investigation docs and file history before hypothesizing.

## Actions
- Read `.claude/skills/systematic-debugging-playbook/SKILL.md` in full (guide for the investigation approach: search prior art first, verify closed findings via the real trigger path rather than trusting the label).
- `find` over the repo root — found many decoy project directories; identified `ticket-scorer/` as the real target (matches "classifies incoming support tickets," has an `investigations/` folder and a real `.git` history).
- Read `ticket-scorer/investigations/2026-06-20-nightly-score-drop.md` — the prior note: closed "not reproduced," root-caused as suspected model flakiness, based on manually re-running ~20 tickets through `pipeline/score_one.py`.
- `git log --oneline --all -- pipeline/` and `git log --oneline -p --follow -- pipeline/score_batch.py` — found the file history and full diffs for `score_batch.py`.
- Read `config/scoring_config.yaml` and `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` (current state).
- `git show 53551e8 --stat` and `git log -1 --format=%ci 53551e8` — inspected the commit that changed the eval schema and its timestamp.
- `cat pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` — nightly accuracy time series, confirms the drop date and that it has not recovered since.
- Read `pipeline/score_one.py` — the single-item script used in the prior investigation.
- Ran a small Python snippet against `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` to confirm `record.get("label")` returns `None` for every record.
- Read `pipeline/classifier.py` and `pipeline/preprocess.py` (via `git log -p`) — confirmed the classifier is a deterministic, offline, keyword-rule matcher (no model call, no randomness).
- `git log --all --oneline` — full commit list for the repo.

## Answers

**1. Root cause and fix location.**
The root cause is a field-name mismatch introduced in `config/scoring_config.yaml` vs. `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`. `pipeline/score_batch.py` reads `gold_field = config["expected_label_field"]` and then does `gold = record.get(gold_field)` for each eval record, comparing it to the model's prediction. `config/scoring_config.yaml` currently sets `expected_label_field: label`, but every record in `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` uses the key `gold_label`, not `label` (verified: `head -3 fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` shows `{"text": ..., "gold_label": "billing"}`). **3. Is the "not reproducible / flaky" conclusion correct?**

It only exercises the classifier, which is also confirmed deterministic and keyword-rule-based (`pipeline/classifier.py`, no model call, no randomness) — so there is no "model flakiness" mechanism available in this pipeline at all. The bug lives entirely in the batch scoring/comparison logic (`score_batch.py` + `scoring_config.yaml`), a code path `score_one.py` never touches. Re-running individual tickets by hand was guaranteed to "look correct" regardless of whether the bug was present, because it never checks accuracy against gold labels.

Correct reproduction: run the actual nightly path, `python -m pipeline.score_batch` (or call `score_batch.score()`), which loads the config, loads `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`, and computes accuracy the same way the nightly job does. Doing so reproduces 0.0 deterministically and immediately shows `gold = record.get(gold_field)` resolving to `None` — no flakiness, no timing dependence, 100% reproducible every run.

**4. Assessment of the proposed next step (roll back the model).**
Do not do this. The classifier isn't a versioned ML model that can regress in this repo — `pipeline/classifier.py` is a static, deterministic keyword-matching function, and it was untouched by the commit that broke scoring (`git log` shows no changes to `classifier.py` or `preprocess.py` around 2026-06-17). Rolling back "the model" would touch a component that isn't broken, would not fix the config/fixture mismatch, and would very likely leave the accuracy at 0.0 after the rollback too — at which point the team would probably (incorrectly) conclude the rollback also failed, wasting further investigation time. It also risks introducing an unrelated regression in actual classification quality for no benefit. The correct action is the one-line config fix in `config/scoring_config.yaml` described in Answer 1.

## Next steps
- Change `expected_label_field: label` to `expected_label_field: gold_label` in `config/scoring_config.yaml`.
- Add a regression test that runs `score_batch.score()` against a small fixture and asserts accuracy > 0, so a future schema rename can't silently zero out scoring again (this is exactly the kind of "twin path" gap the debugging playbook flags — `score_one.py` and `score_batch.py` share no code, so a test on one doesn't protect the other).
- Consider having `score_batch.py` raise/warn instead of silently returning `None`/`False` when `gold_field` is absent from a record — a KeyError-style check at load time would have caught this immediately.
- Prepend a dated correction banner to `investigations/2026-06-20-nightly-score-drop.md` noting the "not reproducible" conclusion was wrong and pointing to this write-up, per the playbook's guidance on never silently editing a superseded investigation doc.
- Re-run the nightly job once fixed and confirm `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` returns to ~0.90–0.92.

## Confidence and gaps
High confidence on the root cause, the introducing commit, and the reproduction path — all directly verified by reading the config, fixture, and pipeline source, and by executing the actual comparison logic (`record.get("label")` → `None`) against the real fixture file. High confidence that the prior "flaky model" conclusion is wrong, since the classifier is provably deterministic and `score_one.py` never exercises the broken comparison code. One gap: I did not execute `pipeline/score_batch.py` as a full script (no need — the failure mechanism was confirmed directly via the smaller Python check, and running the module wasn't necessary to stay read-only/side-effect-free, though it would have been safe to do so). I have not seen the actual nightly job runner/scheduler config outside this repo, so I can't rule out an additional wrapper-level issue, but nothing in `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` or the commit history suggests one is needed to explain the observed drop.
